summary: Julia Evans highlights Etsy's quarterly site performance reports published on their Code as Craft blog, praising them as a model of transparency and engineering rigor. She values their public commitment to performance, detailed graphs, explanations of what internal changes caused specific improvements, and honest discussion of measurement tool tradeoffs. She compiles an index of all historical performance reports dating back to 2011. She also notes that the Etsy performance team is mostly women, with Lara Hogan as Senior Engineering Manager, and links to related talks and resources on web performance culture.
key_points: Etsy's quarterly public performance reports represent a valuable model of transparent engineering culture, combining graphs, root cause analysis, and measurement methodology discussion
Both backend and frontend performance are covered, with Lara Hogan's work on frontend performance specifically highlighted
Public commitments to performance create accountability and drive continuous improvement
The Etsy performance team being majority-women and women-led is presented as both noteworthy and a normal, reasonable thing
money_quotes: It's a public commitment to having good site performance.
They explain what internal changes caused that 25ms drop, and they talk in depth about their measurement tools and what the advantages & disadvantages are of each one.
I would love to make a performance report like this at work.
Of course having majority-women teams and teams led by women is a totally normal & reasonable thing but it made me happy to see.
key_insight: Publishing regular, transparent performance reports with detailed graphs and root cause analysis creates a powerful public accountability mechanism for engineering teams.
